message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives:</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Serologic techniques can serve as a complement to diagnose SARS-CoV-2 infection. The objective of our study was to compare the diagnostic performance of six immunoassays to detect antibodies against SARS-CoV-2: three lateral flow immunoassays (LFAs), one ELISA and two chemiluminescence assays (CLIAs).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods:</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We evaluated three LFAs (
                    <jats:italic>Alltest, One Step and SeroFlash</jats:italic>
                    ), one ELISA (
                    <jats:italic>Dia.Pro</jats:italic>
                    ) and 34 two CLIAs (
                    <jats:italic>Elecsys</jats:italic>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>COV2T</jats:italic>
                    ). To assess the specificity, 60 pre-pandemic sera were 35 used. To evaluate the sensitivity, we used 80 serum samples from patients with 36 positive PCR for SARS-CoV-2. Agreement between techniques was evaluated using the kappa score (
                    <jats:italic>k</jats:italic>
                    ).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    All immunoassays showed a specificity of 100% except for
                    <jats:italic>SeroFlash</jats:italic>
                    (96.7%). Overall sensitivity was 61.3%, 73.8%, 67.5%, 85.9%, 88.0% and 92.0% for
                    <jats:italic>Alltest, One Step, SeroFlash, Dia.Pro, Elecsys</jats:italic>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>COV2T</jats:italic>
                    , respectively. Sensitivity increased throughout the first two weeks from the onset of symptoms, reaching sensitivities over 85% from 14 days for all LFAs, being
                    <jats:italic>One Step</jats:italic>
                    the most sensitive (97.6%), followed by
                    <jats:italic>SeroFlash</jats:italic>
                    (95.1%).
                    <jats:italic>Dia.Pro, Elecsys</jats:italic>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>COV2T</jats:italic>
                    showed sensitivities over 97% from 14 days, being 100% for
                    <jats:italic>COV2T</jats:italic>
                    .
                    <jats:italic>One Step</jats:italic>
                    showed the best agreement results among LFAs, showing excellent agreement with
                    <jats:italic>Dia.Pro</jats:italic>
                    (agreement=94.2%,
                    <jats:italic>k</jats:italic>
                    =0.884),
                    <jats:italic>COV2T</jats:italic>
                    (99.1%,
                    <jats:italic>k</jats:italic>
                    =0.981) and
                    <jats:italic>Elecsys</jats:italic>
                    (97.3%,
                    <jats:italic>k</jats:italic>
                    =0.943).
                    <jats:italic>Dia.Pro, COV2T</jats:italic>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>Elecsys</jats:italic>
                    also showed excellent agreement between them.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    <jats:italic>One Step, Dia.Pro, Elecsys</jats:italic>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>COV2T</jats:italic>
                    obtained the best diagnostic performanc e results. All these techniques showed a specificity of 100% and sensitivities over 97% from 14 days after the onset of symptoms, as well as excellent levels of agreement.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Comparative evaluation of six immunoassays for the detection of antibodies against SARS-CoV-2

reference.unstructured: World Health Organization, Coronavirus disease (COVID-19) Weekly Epidemiological Update and Weekly Operational Update, (n.d.). https://www.who.int/emergencies/diseases/novel-coronavirus-2019/situation-reports (accessed August 19, 2020).

reference.article-title: Laboratory Diagnosis of COVID-19: Current Issues and Challenges

reference.unstructured: Vázquez de la Villa, Prevalence of SARS-CoV-2 in Spain (ENE-COVID): a nationwide, population-based seroepidemiological study, The Lancet. (2020) S0140673620314835. https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(20)31483-5.

A.T. Huang , B. Garcia-Carreras , M.D.T. Hitchings , B. Yang , L. Katzelnick , S.M. Rattigan , B. Borgert , C. Moreno , B.D. Solomon , I. Rodriguez-Barraquer , J. Lessler , H. Salje , D.S. Burke , A. Wesolowski , D.A.T. Cummings , A systematic review of antibody mediated immunity to coronaviruses: antibody kinetics, correlates of protection, and association of antibody responses with severity of disease, (2020). https://doi.org/10.1101/2020.04.14.20065771.

reference.article-title: Profiling Early Humoral Response to Diagnose Novel Coronavirus Disease (COVID-19)
Evaluation of two automated and three rapid lateral flow immunoassays for the detection of anti-SARS-CoV-2 antibodies
Clinical evaluation of serological IgG antibody response on the Abbott Architect for established SARS-CoV-2 infection

reference.unstructured: World Health Organization, Advice on the use of point-of-care immunodiagnostic tests for COVID-19, (n.d.). https://www.who.int/news-room/commentaries/detail/advice-on-the-use-of-point-of-careimmunodiagnostic-tests-for-covid-19 (accessed August 19, 2020).

reference.article-title: Alltest rapid lateral flow immunoassays is reliable in diagnosing SARS-CoV-2 infection from 14 days after symptom onset: A prospective singlecenter study
